checks: Glitch — footage that flickers, pops in, or disappears for a single frame or fraction of a second. Check every frame for sudden scene changes, flashes, or content that appears briefly then cuts away. Even a half-second appearance of wrong footage counts. Flag with timestamp.
Black screen — any frame that is fully black when it should not be. Check every frame, especially the very first and very last.
Dead air — a period of COMPLETE silence with no voice AND no background music. Flag ALL dead_air entries from the audio data with exact timestamps and duration.

Inter-sentence pause — a gap of silence between spoken sentences where the voice stops but music may still be present (music_gap entries in audio data). Flag ALL music_gap entries of 0.5 seconds or longer — every single one listed. These pauses between lines should be trimmed to keep pacing tight. Do not skip any entry, even if there are many.

Audio clipping or distortion — if the audio data shows a clipping/loudness flag, create exactly ONE issue for it. Describe in plain language what the editor hears and what needs fixing. Do not create multiple issues for the same underlying audio problem.

Audio inconsistency — compare audio quality and volume across different sections of the video. Flag if: (1) one section is noticeably louder or quieter than another, (2) background noise or room tone changes between clips (e.g. quiet room suddenly becomes loud), (3) recording quality shifts mid-video (e.g. clean mic suddenly sounds distant or tinny), (4) the audio data shows INCONSISTENCY FLAG. Even if clipping is already flagged separately, inconsistency is a distinct issue — flag both if both exist.
Black lines — horizontal or vertical artifact lines at frame edges or across the image.
Jarring jump cut — an abrupt, disorienting scene break with no transition where the cut feels unintentional or rough.

Off-centered overlay — for EVERY frame that contains any overlay (text card, caption box, logo, lower third, graphic, title, or CTA), ask: is this element horizontally and vertically centered where it should be? Compare its position to the frame edges. If an element is visibly closer to one side, cut off near an edge, or clearly not where it should sit relative to the subject, flag it. Do not skip frames — check each one individually. Even slight but visible misalignment counts.
Misaligned graphic element — any overlay, graphic, or text element that appears skewed, tilted, or not aligned with other elements it should be parallel to.

Endcard missing or incorrect — check the last 5 seconds for a dedicated closing/endcard. Flag if the video ends abruptly with no dedicated endcard frame. Only do NOT flag if there is a clear dedicated endcard in the final seconds. IMPORTANT: A branded black screen with a logo or brand name in white text IS a valid endcard — do not flag it as missing. Examples of valid endcards: black screen with 'IL MAKIAGE NEW YORK' in white text, black screen with brand logo, white screen with brand name. Only flag if the last frames show the talent mid-scene, a freeze frame with no branding, or a plain black/white screen with zero brand elements.
Branding absent — scan the full video for any client logo or brand identifier. Flag if no branding element appears at any point during the video.

VO pronunciation error — review the transcript for words the talent appears to have mispronounced, especially brand names, product names, or medical/technical terms. Flag timestamp, word heard, and what it should be.
do_not_flag: Music gaps under 0.5 seconds — very brief pauses are natural
Subtle alignment differences you are not confident about — only flag clear, obvious misalignment

Caption split typos, caption-VO mismatches, caption typos, spelling errors in captions, or ANY caption text content — these are exclusively handled by the Captions agent. Never flag caption content under Overlays, Video, or any other category
Duplicate captions of any kind — NEVER flag. This check is fully disabled across all agents
Two lines of a single caption — completely normal, never flag
Any caption-related issue under Overlays or Video — captions are exclusively the Captions agent's job
Anything you cannot confirm from the frames or audio data — do not flag based on assumption or inference
